title: The tapestry butterfly bobbin
subtitle: Winding a small hand bobbin of weft yarn for use in tapestry and colour-block weaving
excerpt: A butterfly bobbin is a small figure-8 wind of weft yarn around the fingers, held by a locking loop. It is the standard hand-held weft package for tapestry weaving, where multiple small bobbins replace the shuttle for different colour areas. This guide covers the winding technique and how to use a butterfly bobbin to pack weft into the tapestry shed.
sourceNotes: The butterfly bobbin is a universal tapestry tool used across traditions. Its use in North American and European tapestry practice is observation-led and standard.

content.text: is wound around thumb and little finger in a figure-8. The
holds it together. The yarn feeds from the tail inside the bobbin, not from the outside, which prevents the bobbin from unspooling accidentally while in the shed.

items.name: Tapestry weft yarn (wool, cotton, or linen)

content.text: Hold the yarn tail between thumb and palm. Bring the yarn across the back of the thumb, around the front, across to the little finger, around the back of the little finger, and back to the thumb. This is one figure-8 loop. Continue winding in this pattern until you have used all the yarn, leaving about 15 cm as a tail.

Hold the bobbin at its centre (the crossing point of the figure-8). Wind the remaining tail around the centre of the bobbin three or four times, then push the tail under the last wrap to lock it. The bobbin will now hold together without unrolling.
Step 3: Using the butterfly in the shed

Open the tapestry shed. Push the butterfly bobbin through the shed from one side. The yarn pays off from the inside of the butterfly as you push it across, which keeps the butterfly tangle-free. When packing weft for tapestry, leave the weft in a small arc (not pulled straight) before beating, so the weft has enough length to curve over and under each warp thread without pulling the selvedge in.
Step 4: Releasing yarn from the butterfly

When the butterfly runs short inside the shed, pull the locking-loop tail free and feed more yarn from the butterfly. The figure-8 wind feeds cleanly without tangling as long as the yarn is pulled from the tail inside, not from the outermost wrap.

items.fix: Always feed from the tail. If it has tangled, remove the butterfly from the shed, re-lock the loop, and pass from the inside tail.
items.cause: Yarn was pulled from the outside of the bobbin rather than the tail inside
items.problem: The butterfly unwinds inside the shed and tangles

items.fix: Add two more wraps at the centre before locking. For slippery yarns, finish with a simple overhand knot at the locking wrap.
items.cause: Insufficient wraps around the bobbin centre, or yarn too slippery (silk)
items.problem: The locking loop keeps slipping undone

items.fix: Wind no more than 10-12 m per butterfly. A smaller bobbin passes more easily and is easier to handle.
items.problem: The butterfly is too bulky to pass through the shed
